questions.text: What is the MAXIMUM total supply of Bitcoin that will ever exist?

options.text: Unlimited - new bitcoins can be created by miners forever

questions.explanation: Bitcoin's code hard-codes the maximum supply at exactly 21 million coins. This is a mathematical guarantee from the halving schedule, not a policy that can be changed.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B - 21 million

Bitcoin's code hard-codes the maximum supply at exactly 21 million coins. This is a mathematical guarantee from the halving schedule, not a policy that can be changed.

options.text: An event where the total supply of Bitcoin is cut in half
An event that occurs every 210,000 blocks (~4 years) where the block reward miners receive is reduced by 50%
A process where old bitcoins are destroyed to reduce the circulating supply
A software update that makes transactions twice as fast

questions.explanation: The Halving reduces the number of new bitcoins created per block. It happens automatically every 210,000 blocks (~4 years). The total supply does not get cut - only the rate of new issuance slows.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B - Block reward cut in half every 210,000 blocks

The Halving reduces the number of new bitcoins created per block. It happens automatically every 210,000 blocks (~4 years). The total supply does not get cut - only the rate of new issuance slows.

questions.text: What was the block reward when Bitcoin launched in 2009?
questions.explanation: When Satoshi mined the Genesis Block, miners earned 50 BTC per block. After three halvings (2012, 2016, 2020), the reward has declined to 3.125 BTC (as of 2024).
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: A - 50 BTC

When Satoshi mined the Genesis Block, miners earned 50 BTC per block. After three halvings (2012, 2016, 2020), the reward has declined to 3.125 BTC (as of 2024).

questions.text: What hidden message did Satoshi Nakamoto embed in the Genesis Block?

options.text: "The Times 03/Jan/2009 Chancellor on brink of second bailout for banks"

questions.explanation: Satoshi embedded the front-page headline from The Times (London) as a timestamp and a political statement about the banking system Bitcoin was designed to circumvent.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B - The Times headline

Satoshi embedded the front-page headline from The Times (London) as a timestamp and a political statement about the banking system Bitcoin was designed to circumvent.

questions.text: Why is Bitcoin often called "digital gold"?

options.text: Because it is yellow in color when displayed on charts
Because it shares key properties with gold: scarcity (fixed supply), durability, and the need to "mine" it through hard work
Because it is backed by physical gold stored in vaults
Because its creator, Satoshi, was a gold miner

questions.explanation: Bitcoin's fixed supply (21M), mining process (Proof-of-Work), and role as a store of value mirror gold's monetary properties. It cannot be printed at will.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: B - Shares properties with gold

Bitcoin's fixed supply (21M), mining process (Proof-of-Work), and role as a store of value mirror gold's monetary properties. It cannot be printed at will.

questions.text: Approximately what year will the LAST bitcoin be mined, when the block reward becomes zero?
questions.explanation: Because the block reward halves every 4 years, it will eventually drop below 1 satoshi and become zero. By that time, all 21 million BTC will have been mined. Miners will then rely solely on transaction fees.
questions.modelAnswer:
✅ Correct Answer: C - Around 2140

Because the block reward halves every 4 years, it will eventually drop below 1 satoshi and become zero. By that time, all 21 million BTC will have been mined. Miners will then rely solely on transaction fees.

questions.text: "Bitcoin's supply is controlled by the Bitcoin Foundation, which can increase the 21 million cap if needed to prevent deflation."
questions.explanation: 1 mark: States FALSE and mentions that no central authority controls Bitcoin.+1 mark: Explains that the 21M cap is hard-coded and changing it requires a hard fork that the community would reject.
questions.modelAnswer:
📊 Marks Breakdown

1 mark: States FALSE and mentions that no central authority controls Bitcoin.
+1 mark: Explains that the 21M cap is hard-coded and changing it requires a hard fork that the community would reject.

questions.text: "If fewer miners participate in the Bitcoin network, the block time will become longer than 10 minutes until more miners join."
questions.explanation: 1 mark: States FALSE and mentions the difficulty adjustment.+1 mark: Explains that difficulty adjusts every 2,016 blocks to maintain 10-minute block times, so fewer miners do NOT permanently increase block time.
questions.modelAnswer:
📊 Marks Breakdown

1 mark: States FALSE and mentions the difficulty adjustment.
+1 mark: Explains that difficulty adjusts every 2,016 blocks to maintain 10-minute block times, so fewer miners do NOT permanently increase block time.
